20001.instructions:
Directions for questions 1 to 4 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

Arnold here sums up what is at once so disconcerting yet fascinating about Bronte's poetry in general and the immortality poem in particular: the strange ambivalence of the woman at peace in the domestic serenity of her father's house, yet intellectually rebelling in the private world she fashioned for herself. Incompatible as these facets of her personality appear to be, Emily seemed to have had little difficulty in moving between these two worlds; the solace and enjoyment afforded by the one compensated for the momentary dissatisfactions of the other, and vice versa. This easy discourse between these two realms of existence appears never to have been shattered, even when Emily repudiated the traditional religious creeds which gave solace to man in the storm-troubled sphere of every day existence. We can dismiss this seeming indifference, as so many friends and critics have done, by admitting that she was indeed a law unto herself. But this does not answer the more immediate problem I would like to deal with here, namely Emily Bronte's ability to argue the immortality of the soul in a world of flux without the aid of faith. The first stanza of the poem appears to be orthodox enough. Fear amid the impermanence of the world is allayed by hope in an eternal world beyond.

The poetry as a whole gives ample evidence of Heaven's ability and willingness to dispel man's timidity. Kind, merciful, and just, God assures happiness to those who persevere. At least this is the comforting illusion traditional religion asks us to accept as the solution to the problem of evil in the world. Even "A God of hate could hardly bear / To watch through all eternity / His own creation's dread despair!". It is unreasonable to assume, in other words, that the long agony of human existence will be punished with eternal tears. The frightening thing about this conclusion, however, is that it is not only unwarranted, but ineffectual in dispelling fear. There is no evidence supporting our belief in merciful God. Empirically, it is just as easy to prove the existence of a God of hate as it is to prove the existence of a God of love. For as surely as heaven shines arming man from fear, hell shines disarming him of joy.

But the important question here is not the nature of God or man's position in the cosmos relative to heaven or hell. These considerations aggravate rather than illuminate man's perplexity. The significant point is the narrator's sudden and unexplained discovery of personal contentment in the storm-troubled sphere generated in large measure by these questions as well as by the cladogenetic character of nature itself. Subject to the destructive forces of space and time, the physical world answers man's request for permanence, certainty, and love with doubt, suspicion, and hate. "So hopeless is the world without, / The world within I doubly prize". It is therefore within the very soul of man, rather than in the physical world, that man must discover the permanent grounds of existence in general. Were it not for the realization of God's presence within, the narrator would remain a coward soul. It is this divine indwelling which calms the soul.
20001.question_text: Which of the following can be inferred from the given passage? 1
options: Failing to discover contentment in the fragmented world, the soul must find God within itself.
Not only does God dwell in man, but also man dwells in God.
The very identities of man and God are dependent upon one another.
God is that being in whom the individual species of creation realize peace and permanence through the denial of their ego.
20001.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1
Refer to the lines - "... the storm-troubled sphere ... the physical world answers man's request for permanence, certainty, and love with doubt, suspicion, and hate ... It is therefore within the very soul of man, rather than in the physical world, that man must discover the permanent grounds of existence in general. Were it not for the realization of God's presence within ... this divine indwelling ..." Thus, option (1) is the correct answer.

Directions for questions 1 to 4 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

"So hopeless is the world without, / The world within I doubly prize". It is therefore within the very soul of man, rather than in the physical world, that man must discover the permanent grounds of existence in general. Were it not for the realization of God's presence within, the narrator would remain a coward soul. It is this divine indwelling which calms the soul.
20002.question_text: Which of the following is true about Bronte’s poetry? 1
options: The poem undermines rather than affirms the hope that this world can become a tolerable place.
The poem as a whole contradicts itself regarding the very point it apparently attempts to establish.
The poem suggests that faith is essential to achieve immortality in a world of flux.
None of these
20002.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
Refer to the lines “…namely Emily Bronte’s ability to argue the immortality of the soul in a world of flux without the aid of faith. The first stanza of the poem appears to be orthodox enough. Fear amid the impermanence of the world is allayed by hope in an eternal world beyond … The significant point is the narrator’s sudden and unexplained discovery of personal contentment … “So hopeless is the world without, / The world within I doubly prize”. It is therefore within the very soul of man, rather than in the physical world, that man must discover the permanent grounds of existence in general.” Bronte’s poetry first suggests that hope in the eternal world would lead to contentment but later indicates that one must look within to achieve contentment. Hence, option (2) is the correct answer.

20003.instructions:
Directions for questions 1 to 4 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

20003.question_text: The tone of the author is 1
options: elucidatory
analytical
contemplative
narrative
20003.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
The author analyses Bronte's poem and the contradiction that it presents. Hence, option (2) is the correct answer.

20004.instructions:
Directions for questions 5 to 8 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

King Lear is the bare skeleton of the play, and even here Tolstoy wants to cut out most of what is essential. He objects to the storm, as being unnecessary, to the Fool, who in his eyes is simply a tedious nuisance and an excuse for making bad jokes, and to the death of Cordelia, which, as he sees it, robs the play of its moral. It is perhaps more significant that Tolstoy sees no justification for the presence of the Fool. The Fool is integral to the play. He acts not only as a sort of chorus, making the central situation clearer by commenting on it more intelligently than the other characters, but as a foil to Lear’s frenzies. His jokes, riddles and scraps of rhyme, and his endless digs at Lear’s high-minded folly, ranging from mere derision to a sort of melancholy poetry (‘All thy other titles thou hast given away, that thou wast born with’), are like a trickle of sanity running through the play, a reminder that somewhere or other, in spite of the injustices, cruelties, intrigues, deceptions and misunderstandings that are being enacted here, life is going on much as usual. In Tolstoy’s impatience with the Fool, one gets a glimpse of his deeper quarrel with Shakespeare. He objects, with some justification, to the raggedness of Shakespeare’s plays, the irrelevancies, the incredible plots, the exaggerated language: but what at bottom he probably most dislikes is a sort of exuberance, a tendency to take not so much a pleasure as simply an interest in the actual process of life. It is a mistake to write Tolstoy off as a moralist attacking an artist. He never said that art, as such, is wicked or meaningless, nor did he even say that technical virtuosity is unimportant. But his main aim, in his later years, was to narrow the range of human consciousness. One’s interests, one’s points of attachment to the physical world and the day-to-day struggle, must be as few, and not as many, as possible. Literature must consist of parables, stripped of detail and almost independent of language. The parables — this is where Tolstoy differs from the average vulgar puritan — must themselves be works of art, but pleasure and curiosity must be excluded from them. Science, also, must be divorced from curiosity. The business of science, he says, is not to discover what happens but to teach men how they ought to live. So, too, with history and politics. Many problems (for example, the Dreyfus case) are simply not worth solving, and he is willing to leave them as loose ends. Indeed, his whole theory of ‘crazes’ or ‘epidemic suggestions’, in which he lumps together such things as the Crusades and the Dutch passion of tulip growing, shows a willingness to regard many human activities as mere ant-like rushings to and fro, inexplicable and uninteresting. Clearly, he could have no patience with a chaotic, detailed, discursive writer like Shakespeare. His reaction is that of an irritable old man who is being pestered by a noisy child. ‘Why do you keep jumping up and down like that? Why can’t you sit still like I do?’ In a way the old man is in the right, but the trouble is that the child has a feeling in its limbs which the old man has lost. And while the old man knows of the existence of this feeling, the effect is merely to increase his irritation.
20004.question_text: Which of the following is most likely to be the source of the passage? 1
options: An editorial of a newspaper
An autobiography of Bronte
A book review
A journal
20004.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
The author presents a thorough analysis of Bronte's poem. This style of writing is best suited to a journal. Hence, option (4) is the correct answer.

20005.instructions:
Directions for questions 5 to 8 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

20005.question_text: Which of the following can be inferred from the passage? 1
options: Tolstoy is a typical puritan who treats Shakespeare, not as a poet, but as a dramatist.
Tolstoy’s treatment of Shakespeare is faulty because of Tolstoy’s own limited ability.
Tolstoy might be justified in saying that King Lear is not a very good play.
Tolstoy fails to realize that he is unable to appreciate the deeper nuances of Shakespeare’s writings.
20005.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Refer to the lines “King Lear is the bare skeleton of the play … He objects, with some justification, to the raggedness of Shakespeare’s plays …” This makes option (3) correct. The passage mentions that Tolstoy is different from the average vulgar puritan. This makes option (1) incorrect. Tolstoy’s limited view of Shakespeare is not a comment on Tolstoy’s ability but on his rigidity. This makes option (2) incorrect. Option (4) is incorrect as the passage states that Tolstoy is aware that he misses something in Shakespeare that others are able to see and appreciate – “…And while the old man knows of the existence of this feeling … “ Thus, option (3) is the correct answer.

Directions for questions 5 to 8 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

20006.question_text: Which of the following can be inferred from the passage? 1
options: Artistic theories such as Tolstoy’s are quite worthless as ultimately there is no test of literary merit except survival, which is itself an index of majority opinion.
Artistic theories such as Tolstoy’s start out with arbitrary assumptions about what ‘good’ literature must have.
Artistic theories such as Tolstoy’s are quite worthless because they depend on vague terms that can be interpreted in any way one chooses.
Artistic theories such as Tolstoy’s are quite worthless because they fail to realize that the objective of art is different from the objective of science.
20006.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
Refer to the lines “…his main aim, in his later years, was to narrow the range of human consciousness … Literature must consist of parables, stripped of detail and almost independent of language. The parables — this is where Tolstoy differs from the average vulgar puritan — must themselves be works of art, but pleasure and curiosity must be excluded from them.” Option (2) follows from this and hence, is the correct answer.

20007.question_text: Which of the following is true of Tolstoy? 1
options: Tolstoy sought to detach literature from pleasure and curiosity.
Tolstoy is aware that he misses something in Shakespeare.
For Tolstoy, the purpose of science is not very different from the purpose of literature.
All of the above
20007.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
Refer to the lines "… but pleasure and curiosity must be excluded from them. Science, also, must be divorced from curiosity." Options (1) and (3) can be inferred from these lines. Option (2) can be inferred from the lines "And the old man knows of the existence of this feeling …" Thus, option (4) is the correct answer.

20008.instructions:
Directions for questions 9 to 12 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

In the classic despotisms, everything in society was the private property of the despot, but in the modern world, this basic distinction has been steadily eroded from the other side: ever larger areas of private life have come to be publicly regulated. If everything controversial is called ‘political’, and if the personal is the political, then nothing is left outside the scope of control by government. This argument has not been universally accepted, but it has been the basic premise of twentieth-century totalitarianism, and its effect is clearly to lock the individual within a single system of control, destroying the inheritance of distinct and independent roles (economic, religious, cultural, social, and legal) which modern states have, until recently, enjoyed. Slogans such as ‘the personal is the political’ are proposals for action disguised as truths about the world. The meaning of such slogans is often obscure, but they contain sleeping implications which may awaken in new circumstances and demand policies which affront other cherished values, such as individual freedom.

It is said that the price of freedom is vigilance, and an important form of vigilance is attention to political rhetoric, which often reveals how things are going. The beginning of wisdom in politics is attention to signs of change. As a theatre of illusion, politics does not reveal its meanings to the careless eye. Reality and illusion are central categories of political study. The problem begins with the very names of institutions. The dominance of Western fashions means that every country now has a kind of politics, and a complement of institutions – parliaments, constitutions, schedules of rights, trade unions, courts, newspapers, ministers, and so on – which suggests that the same kind of thing is going on all over the world. Nothing could be further from the truth. Japan, for example, has a figure called a prime minister, and many mistakes have been made by foreign statesmen who are frustrated to discover that the Japanese prime minister cannot deliver national policies in the way that rulers of other countries sometimes can. Again, in 1936, Stalin promulgated what was widely claimed to be the most advanced constitution in the world, awash with rights and safeguards for the people of the Soviet Union. The reality was that Stalin was at that very moment engaged in ‘purging’ the Soviet élite through fake trials. His subjects were being shot by the million. It is not unknown for politicians to lie, but much more confusing is the complex relation between names and reality. Above all, the name of politics itself. When concepts are stretched too far, they snap and lose their usefulness. ‘Politics’ used to refer merely to the actions of monarchs, parliaments, and ministers, and to the activities of the politically committed who helped or hindered their accession to authority. Everything else was social or private life. With the expansion of the power of governments, nearly everything has come to be described, in one way or another, as ‘political’. We may mention here just one of the many reasons for this. Governments wishing to claim credit for all good things, and oppositions wishing to dispense blame for all bad things, have colluded in spreading the idea that all things, good and bad, are caused by political policies. This idea can turn subjects into petitioners to the government, from which all benefits seem to flow, and that in turn reinforces the idea that everything actually is political. There is another reason why politics has expanded its role and meaning. It has, from times immemorial in Europe, been the business of monarchs and their servants, and history has largely been the narrative of their doings. To participate in politics has thus been to attain a kind of immortality.
20008.question_text: Which of the following is true of the author of this passage? 1
options: The author is a staunch supporter of Shakespeare.
The author is contemptuous towards Tolstoy because of Tolstoy’s criticism of Shakespeare.
The author appreciates Shakespeare.
The author urges Tolstoy to see reason.
20008.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Only option (3) can be inferred from the passage. Option (1) is incorrect because although the author appreciates Shakespeare, he does not appear to be a 'staunch' supporter. Option (2) is incorrect as the author criticizes Tolstoy but does not show contempt. Option (4) is also not supported by the passage. Thus, option (3) is the correct answer.

20009.instructions:
Directions for questions 9 to 12 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

In the classic despotisms, everything in society was the private property of the despot, but in the modern world, this basic distinction has been steadily eroded from the other side: ever larger areas of private life have come to be publicly regulated. If everything controversial is called ‘political’, and if the personal is the political, then nothing is left outside the scope of control by government. This argument has not been universally accepted, but it has been the basic premise of twentieth-century totalitarianism, and its effect is clearly to lock the individual within a single system of control, destroying the inheritance of distinct and independent roles (economic, religious, cultural, social, and legal) which modern states have, until recently, enjoyed. Slogans such as ‘the personal is the political’ are proposals for action disguised as truths about the world. The meaning of such slogans is often obscure, but they contain sleeping implications which may awaken in new circumstances and demand policies which affront other cherished values, such as individual freedom.

It is said that the price of freedom is vigilance, and an important form of vigilance is attention to political rhetoric, which often reveals how things are going. The beginning of wisdom in politics is attention to signs of change. As a theatre of illusion, politics does not reveal its meanings to the careless eye. Reality and illusion are central categories of political study. The problem begins with the very names of institutions. The dominance of Western fashions means that every country now has a kind of politics, and a complement of institutions – parliaments, constitutions, schedules of rights, trade unions, courts, newspapers, ministers, and so on – which suggests that the same kind of thing is going on all over the world. Nothing could be further from the truth. Japan, for example, has a figure called a prime minister, and many mistakes have been made by foreign statesmen who are frustrated to discover that the Japanese prime minister cannot deliver national policies in the way that rulers of other countries sometimes can. Again, in 1936, Stalin promulgated what was widely claimed to be the most advanced constitution in the world, awash with rights and safeguards for the people of the Soviet Union. The reality was that Stalin was at that very moment engaged in ‘purging’ the Soviet élite through fake trials. His subjects were being shot by the million. It is not unknown for politicians to lie, but much more confusing is the complex relation between names and reality. Above all, the name of politics itself. When concepts are stretched too far, they snap and lose their usefulness. ‘Politics’ used to refer merely to the actions of monarchs, parliaments, and ministers, and to the activities of the politically committed who helped or hindered their accession to authority. Everything else was social or private life. With the expansion of the power of governments, nearly everything has come to be described, in one way or another, as ‘political’. We may mention here just one of the many reasons for this. Governments wishing to claim credit for all good things, and oppositions wishing to dispense blame for all bad things, have colluded in spreading the idea that all things, good and bad, are caused by political policies. This idea can turn subjects into petitioners to the government, from which all benefits seem to flow, and that in turn reinforces the idea that everything actually is political. There is another reason why politics has expanded its role and meaning. It has, from times immemorial in Europe, been the business of monarchs and their servants, and history has largely been the narrative of their doings. To participate in politics has thus been to attain a kind of immortality.
20009.question_text: The author of the passage is most likely to agree with which of the following? 1
options: We are free and tolerant in our private lives; but in public affairs we must abide by the law.
The boundary between what is public and what is private is changing.
The private world is that of the family, and of individual conscience, as each individual makes his or her own choice of beliefs and interests.
The essence of political life can be found only once the boundary between private and political life is devalued as illusion.
20009.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
Refer to the lines “’Politics’ used to refer merely to the actions of monarchs, parliaments, and ministers … With the expansion of the power of governments, nearly everything has come to be described, in one way or another, as ‘political’.” Option (2) follows from this and hence, is the correct answer.

Slogans such as ‘the personal is the political’ are proposals for action disguised as truths about the world. 20010.question_text: Which of the following can be inferred from the passage? 1
options: Recognizing a division between what is public and what is private is what distinguishes politics from despotism.
To understand politics, one must include the signs which might tell us what is going on beneath the surface.
Political speeches often reveal how things are going on under the surface.
Vigilance is the key to success in the political arena.
20010.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
Refer to the lines “It is said that the price of freedom is vigilance, and an important form … does not reveal its meanings to the careless eye. Reality and illusion are central categories of political study.” Hence, option (2) is the correct answer.

20011.instructions:
Directions for questions 9 to 12 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

This idea can turn subjects into petitioners to the government, from which all benefits seem to flow, and that in turn reinforces the idea that everything actually is political. There is another reason why politics has expanded its role and meaning. It has, from times immemorial in Europe, been the business of monarchs and their servants, and history has largely been the narrative of their doings. To participate in politics has thus been to attain a kind of immortality.
20011.question_text: Which of the following examples are in line with the following lines from the passage “‘Politics’ used to refer merely to the actions of monarchs, parliaments, and ministers, and to …….., nearly everything has come to be described, in one way or another, as ‘political’”? A. Homosexuality and religion, which used to be publicly regulated, are now largely private affairs. B. It has become routine to hear about promising political careers imperiled by revelations of extramarital sex. C. In a presidential debate, questions were asked about the upbringing of the children of the respective candidates. 1
options: A and B
B and C
C and A
All of the above
20011.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
The author of the passage argues that ever larger areas have become political. Statements B and C are examples where private life has become a part of politics. Thus, option (2) is the correct answer.

20012.instructions:
Directions for questions 13 to 16 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

The Greeks were humanists, but of a kind strikingly different from the humanism (transformed by Christianity) found in the modern world. Their basic proposition was that man is a rational animal, and that the meaning of human life is found in the exercise of rationality. When men succumbed to the passions, they were shamefully descending to a lower form of being. When pride, or hubris, led them to think they were gods, they lost sight of their human limitations and suffered nemesis, the destructive resentment of the gods. The secret of life was human self-knowledge, and a balanced expression of one’s human capacities. In deliberating about law and public policy, man found his highest and purest form of self-expression. It could only be enjoyed in the political life of a city.

Humanists often look to the Greeks as ancestors, but their view of the world has one remarkable (and in modern terms, disturbing) implication. Since some are less rational than others, so also are they less human. Slaves in particular are defective in rationality when compared to masters. Those who explored this view, above all the philosopher Aristotle, were perfectly aware that some slaves are clever and some masters stupid; they were merely expounding what they took to be the rational foundation of the institution itself. Again, women and children were taken to be less rational than men, though Aristotle considered the barbarians to be quite wrong in thinking that they were indistinguishable from slaves. Citizenship was thus confined to free adult males, and in some cities not even to all of those. The activity of politics and that of waging war merged with each other, and it thus seemed natural that women should live domestic lives: they could hardly stand their ground in a phalanx. It might seem that, in taking this view, the Greeks were the prisoners of the prejudices of their time.

Being immensely imaginative in their exploration of the world, however, they had no difficulty imagining women doing any number of things: becoming warriors in the form of Amazons; going on a sexual strike to enforce peace in the Lysistrata of Aristophanes; taking the role of philosopher–rulers in the Guardians of Plato’s Republic; but these images were not the reality of everyday life.

The laws and policies of a Greek city emerged, then, not from the palace of a despot, but from discussion among notionally equal citizens in the agora, the market-place which also generally served as the arena of politics. Citizens enjoyed equality before the law (isonomia, a term sometimes used as a synonym for democracy) and an equal opportunity to speak in the assembly. In a large city such as Athens, thousands of people might turn up to such meetings, so that the speakers were predominantly aristocrats who had studied the art of speaking, or notable leaders who had managed to acquire a band of supporters. In democracies, many offices were filled by lot, but the main officers were elected and were commonly from powerful families.

In Thucidides’ history of the Peloponnesian War we can see the democratic process at work – for example, in the Athenian debate, reported in Book III, as to how the people of Mitylene, who had rebelled against Athenian hegemony, should be punished. In this debate, the popular leader Cleon put the case for confirming the decision already taken - to kill the men and sell the women and children into slavery.

Cleon appealed to realism: if you want to have an empire, he argued, you must be prepared to do the ruthless things necessary to keep it together. His opponent, Diodotus, argued for clemency on the ground that ruthlessness would merely turn every occasion of revolt among the clients of Athens into a life and death struggle. It was Diodotus who triumphed in this vivid intellectual contest.
20012.question_text: Which of the following concurs with the author’s view on the role of politics? 1
options: The definition of politics has expanded to become all-encompassing.
The definition of politics used to be controlled by the actions of monarchs and government officials.
The idea that everything is political is only an illusion created by politicians.
It is often believed that all things good or bad are caused by political policies.
20012.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
Refer to the lines “have colluded in spreading the idea that all things, good and bad, are caused by political policies.” This makes option (4) the correct answer.

20013.instructions:
Directions for questions 13 to 16 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

20013.question_text: According to the passage, which of the following is not a system of orderly subordination that would have been acceptable to Greeks? 1
options: Slave to Master
Woman to Man
Vendor to customer
Child to father
20013.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
All of the above combinations, except for option (3), have been mentioned in the passage. The relationship of vendor and customer can exist in intellectually equal male adults. Thus, option (3) is the correct answer.

20014.instructions:
Directions for questions 13 to 16 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

20014.question_text: How, according to the author, were the Greeks different from Christians? A. The Greeks believed that man is rational. B. The Greeks believed that the meaning of human life is found in the exercise of rationality. C. The Greeks believed that reason is stronger than anger. 1
options: A and B
B and C
C and A
None of the above
20014.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
The passage states “The Greeks were humanists, but of a kind strikingly different from the humanism (transformed by Christianity) found in the modern world.” It goes on to describe Greek humanism. But it does not say anything about the difference between Greeks and Christians. Hence, option (4) is the correct answer.

20015.instructions:
Directions for questions 13 to 16 : The passage given below is followed by a set of four questions. Choose the most appropriate answer to each question.

20015.question_text: If the information given in the passage is true, then which of the following must also be true? 1
options: All political decisions in Greece were taken by the entire population of adult males.
Greek democracy encompassed only a part of the population.
The Greeks agreed that it is sometimes necessary to take ruthless decisions in order to maintain power.
Every decision in Greece was a matter of intellectual conflict among notionally equal citizens.
20015.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
The passage states that Greek democracy did not include women and slaves. Hence, option (2) is the correct answer.

20016.instructions: Directions for question (17): The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, and 4) given in this question, when properly sequenced, form a coherent paragraph. Decide on the proper order for the sentences and key in this sequence of four numbers as your answer.
20016.question_text: The primary purpose of the passage is to 1
options: analyze the political structure and prejudices of the Greeks.
comment on the democratic structure of the Greeks.
comment on the condition of women in Greek societies.
describe the humanism of the Greeks
20016.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
Options (1), (2) and (3) are incomplete as they capture only one aspect of the passage. Refer to the lines “The Greeks were humanists … Their basic proposition was that man is a rational animal”. The passage then goes on to describe Greek humanism and its implications. Hence, option (4) is the correct answer.

20017.instructions: Directions for question (18): The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures the author’s position.
20017.question_text:
1. The extinctions coincided with increased volcanic activity in Siberia. 2. MIT’s Daniel Rothman and colleagues presented compelling evidence that an unprecedented increase in methane-producing microbes is the cause. 3. The cause of the event has long been a topic of debate. 4. But evidence has not supported this as a sole cause.
  • Answer key/Solution
20017.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3142
The strongest clue in this question is sentence 4. ‘This’ in 4 refers to ‘increased volcanic activity in Siberia’ in 1. So, 14 is a pair. ‘Sole cause’ in 4 ensures that 2 will come after 4. So, 142 is a sequence. 3 can only come at the start. 142 explain this ‘debate’. So, 3142 is the correct order.

20018.instructions: Directions for question (18): The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures the author’s position.
20018.question_text: A remarkably intact fossil of a 125-million-year-old mammal provides the earliest record of hair structures and inner organs. Through a rare mode of fossilization, the chipmunk-sized animal, named Spinolestes xenarthrosus, was found with guard hairs, underfur, hedgehog-like spines, and even evidence of a fungal skin infection. Soft tissues that were preserved included those of the earlobe, liver, lung, diaphragm, and skin. Found in Spain, S. xenarthrosus ran in a lush and diverse wetland of the Cretaceous period and belongs to an extinct mammalian branch, the triconodonts. The finding reveals that the hair and skin structures of modern mammals were present even in such early members of this lineage. 1
options: A 125 year old mammal provides proof that the hair and skin structures of modern mammals were present even in such early members of this lineage.
A remarkably intact skeleton of an early mammal helps counter an evolutionary myth that has been propagated for generations.
A fossil belonging to a 125-million-year-old mammal of an extinct branch reveals that members of this lineage had some physiological similarities with modern mammals.

Soft tissues, earlobe, liver, lung, diaphragm, and skin recovered from a 125 year old mammal fossil belonging to the triconodonts branch, now extinct, shows that the hair and skin of modern mammals were present in early members of the human lineage.
20018.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
This can be answered only by the method of elimination as two options are really close. Option 1 is a trap. It says mammal whereas the paragraph talks about the fossil of the mammal. The meanings are very different. Option 2 talks about an ‘evolutionary myth’ which is out of scope. Option 4 is unnecessarily verbose and it distorts the meaning of the paragraph by creating a wrong cause-effect relationship. Only option 3 is relevant. This is the correct answer.

20019.instructions: Directions for question (20): The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, and 4) given in this question, when properly sequenced, form a coherent paragraph. Decide on the proper order for the sentences and key in this sequence of four numbers as your answer.
20019.question_text: There is a sentence missing in the paragraph below. Look at the paragraph and decide in which blank (option 1, 2, 3, or 4) the following sentence would best fit. Sentence : "Today, the world is facing an unprecedented biodiversity crisis, with species disappearing at an alarming rate." Paragraph : Biodiversity, the variety of life on Earth, plays a crucial role in maintaining the balance of ecosystems and supporting the well-being of all living organisms. _______(1). However, human activities, such as deforestation, habitat destruction, pollution, and climate change, have been posing significant threats to biodiversity. ________(2). The loss of biodiversity can have far-reaching consequences, impacting food security, human health, and the stability of ecosystems. ________(3). Conservation efforts and sustainable practices are essential to address the biodiversity crisis. _______(4). Governments, organizations, and individuals must work together to protect species and their habitats, promote responsible consumption, and mitigate the factors contributing to biodiversity loss. 1
options: Option 1
Option 2
Option 3
Option 4
20019.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
The missing sentence "Today, the world is facing an unprecedented biodiversity crisis, with species disappearing at an alarming rate." would best fit in blank option 3.

Paragraph with the correct placement of the sentence:
Biodiversity, the variety of life on Earth, plays a crucial role in maintaining the balance of ecosystems and supporting the well-being of all living organisms. (1). However, human activities, such as deforestation, habitat destruction, pollution, and climate change, have been posing significant threats to biodiversity. Today, the world is facing an unprecedented biodiversity crisis, with species disappearing at an alarming rate. (3). The loss of biodiversity can have far-reaching consequences, impacting food security, human health, and the stability of ecosystems. (4). Conservation efforts and sustainable practices are essential to address the biodiversity crisis. Governments, organizations, and individuals must work together to protect species and their habitats, promote responsible consumption, and mitigate the factors contributing to biodiversity loss.

The sentence when placed in blank 3 provides crucial information about the current state of biodiversity, emphasizing the severity of the crisis with species disappearing rapidly. It complements the context provided in the previous sentence by highlighting the threats to biodiversity due to human activities, and it sets the stage for discussing the consequences of biodiversity loss and the need for conservation efforts in the paragraph.

20020.instructions: Directions for question (21): The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures the author’s position.
20020.question_text:
1. Until 2011, radiokrypton dating was intractable, because of the sheer amount of ice required to detect such trace amounts. 2. This new method opens the possibility of studying the transition from shorter to longer glacial cycles. 3. Advances in detector technology reduced the amount of ice required to the feasible, though large, amount of 350 kilograms. 4. Krypton is a noble gas found in trace amounts in the atmosphere, and krypton-81 is formed when cosmic rays collide with krypton isotopes.
  • Answer key/Solution
20020.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4132
This is a narrative paragraph. So, we need to keep the theme of the paragraph in mind. The best clue is ‘this’ in sentence 2. It refers to the ‘advances’ mentioned in 3. So, 32 is a mandatory pair. Now, thematically, 1 has to come before both 2 and 3 as it talks about a past scenario. 4 is the definition of Krypton or some kind of introduction. It is a classic example of a topic sentence. Hence, 4132 is the correct sequence.

20021.instructions: Directions for question (22): The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures the author’s position.
20021.question_text: Either way, misogyny is still thriving. When the Guardian began researching the online harassment of its own writers, they discovered something bleak: of the 10 contributors who receive the most abuse in the comment threads, eight are women – five white, three non-white – and the other two are black men. Overall, women Guardian writers get more abuse than men, regardless of what they write about, but especially when they write about rape and feminism. 1
options: Women are more abused than men.
Online bullying is a harsh truth of the modern world.
When it comes to online harassment or negative comments, women outnumber men.
A research showed that women are more likely to be abused online than men.
20021.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
The paragraph talks about online harassment. So, option 1 can be eliminated for being too generic. Option 2 is only partially correct. It doesn’t talk about the research and its finding. Option 3 is distorted. The paragraph says women outnumber men in terms of being victims of online harassment. This option portrays them in an ambiguous manner. Moreover, the paragraph is concerned only about the journalists of Guardian. So, option 4 is the correct answer.

20022.instructions: Directions for question (22): The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures the author’s position.
20022.question_text: Accurately or not, if you assess the immediately menacing force as something you potentially have the power to defeat, you go into fight mode. In such instances, the hormones released by your sympathetic nervous system—especially adrenaline—prime you to do battle and, hopefully, triumph over the hostile entity. Conversely, if you view the antagonistic force as too powerful to overcome, your impulse is to outrun it (and the faster the better). And this, of course, is the flight response, also linked to the instantaneous ramping up of your emergency biochemical supplies—so that, ideally, you can escape from this adversarial power (whether it be human, animal, or some calamity of nature). 1
options: The fight or flight response in humans is dependent on the physical perception of threat.
The assessment of one’s immediate threat leads to the fight or flight response.
Humans have two ways of dealing with any dangerous situation: fight or flight.
The fight or flight response mechanism in species is interlinked with evolutionary physiological processes.
20022.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2
The paragraph says that people have two responses to imminent danger: flight or fight. It doesn’t say that these are the only two responses. So, option 3 is out. Option 1 is partially correct. The paragraph says that people react differently, and their physical symptoms are different. So, this option is a little vague. Option 4 is out of scope. Option 2 is the correct answer.

20023.instructions: Directions for question (24): The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, and 4) given in this question, when properly sequenced, form a coherent paragraph. Decide on the proper order for the sentences and key in this sequence of four numbers as your answer.
20023.question_text: There is a sentence missing in the paragraph below. Look at the paragraph and decide in which blank (option 1, 2, 3, or 4) the following sentence would best fit. Sentence : "Mismanagement, increased competition, and financial difficulties led to the downfall of BPL, once a prominent consumer electronics brand." Paragraph : BPL, a renowned consumer electronics brand, had its heyday in the 1990s and early 2000s, known for producing quality televisions, audio systems, and home appliances.____(1)____. However, as time passed, the company faced several challenges that impacted its performance. ___(2)____. The market became saturated with new competitors, offering similar products at competitive prices.____ (3)_____. Unfortunately, these factors culminated in the decline of BPL as a major player in the consumer electronics industry. ____(4)____. Despite its downfall, BPL's legacy continues to be remembered by many consumers who once relied on the brand for innovative and reliable electronics. 1
options: Option 1
Option 2
Option 3
Option 4
20023.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
The missing sentence "Mismanagement, increased competition, and financial difficulties led to the downfall of BPL, once a prominent consumer electronics brand." would best fit in blank option 3.

Paragraph with the correct placement of the sentence:
BPL, a renowned consumer electronics brand, had its heyday in the 1990s and early 2000s, known for producing quality televisions, audio systems, and home appliances. (1). However, as time passed, the company faced several challenges that impacted its performance. Mismanagement, increased competition, and financial difficulties led to the downfall of BPL, once a prominent consumer electronics brand. (3).
The market became saturated with new competitors, offering similar products at competitive prices. (4). Unfortunately, these factors culminated in the decline of BPL as a major player in the consumer electronics industry.
Despite its downfall, BPL's legacy continues to be remembered by many consumers who once relied on the brand for innovative and reliable electronics.
The sentence when placed in blank 3, provides further information about the factors that led to the downfall of BPL, including mismanagement, increased competition, and financial difficulties. It supports the context provided in the preceding sentences, the history and challenges, explaining the specific reasons behind the brand's decline in the consumer electronics market.

20024.instructions: Directions for question (24): The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, and 4) given in this question, when properly sequenced, form a coherent paragraph. Decide on the proper order for the sentences and key in this sequence of four numbers as your answer.
20024.question_text:
1. Marine biologist Denise Risch of NOAA Northeast Fisheries Science Center unwittingly discovered that the sound came from Antarctic minke whales (Balaenoptera bonaerensis) when she and her colleagues tracked two with tags that included underwater microphones. 2. Seafarers have been puzzled for decades by a sound picked up in the oceans around Antarctica, fondly called the “bio-duck.” 3. The discovery opens up new possibilities for tracking these whales and also presents a way to retroactively study their behavior from past recordings. 4. The noises are prevalent throughout the Southern Ocean and also around western Australia.
  • Answer key/Solution
20024.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2413
This is a very easy question. ‘The discovery’ in 3 refers to ‘unwittingly discovered…’ in 1. ‘The noise’ in 4 refers to ‘bio-duck’ in 2. So, 13 and 24 are two mandatory pairs. 2 can’t follow 3. But 2 can be the topic sentence of the paragraph. Hence, 2413 is the correct sequence.
